8030	[Push--pull on a binary coproduct of two legs is the product of the two leg push--pulls]
8031	  \label{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}
8032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_binary_coprod_prod,
8033	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_prodLift_of_isLimit,
8034	    AlgebraicGeometry.coprodDecompMap}
8035	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib,
8036	    lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib, lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}
8037	  Let \(Y_0, Y_1 : \operatorname{Over} X\) be two objects of the slice category over a scheme \(X\),
8038	  and form the binary coproduct of their underlying schemes \(Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\)
8039	  with structure map
8040	  \(\operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom} : Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg
8041	  Y_1.\mathrm{left} \to X\). Then the push--pull object on this binary coproduct is the binary product
8042	  of the two per-leg push--pull objects:
8043	  \[
8044	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,\Bigl(\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(
8045	      \operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\bigr)\Bigr)
8046	      \;\cong\;
8047	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0 \;\times\; \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_1
8048	    \qquad \text{in } X.\mathrm{Modules}.
8049	  \]
8050	\end{lemma}
8051	\begin{proof}
8052	  \uses{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib,
8053	    lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib, lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}
8054	  % NOTE: two Lean-level formalization obstacles the mathematical sketch below omits, with the
8055	  % fixes handed off by the prover who reduced this node:
8056	  %  (a) INSTANCE TRAP. The naive functor `toPresheaf (A ⨿ B) ⋙ (evaluation _ Ab).obj V` gets NO
8057	  %      composite `PreservesLimitsOfShape` instance: `toPresheaf`'s codomain `TopCat.Presheaf Ab`
8058	  %      is a `def` wrapper, so `comp_preservesLimitsOfShape` cannot unify the middle category. FIX:
8059	  %      use the single functor `SheafOfModules.evaluation V` (lands in `ModuleCat`, has a direct
8060	  %      `PreservesLimitsOfShape` instance), and reduce the iso-check via
8061	  %      `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app` (ModuleCat-valued `.app`) — NOT
8062	  %      `NatIso.isIso_of_isIso_app` on `toPresheaf`.
8063	  %  (b) Ab→ModuleCat BRIDGE. `TopCat.Sheaf.isProductOfDisjoint` produces the disjoint-union limit in
8064	  %      `Ab`. To feed it to `isIso_prodLift_of_isLimit` in `ModuleCat`, reflect the Ab-limit through
8065	  %      `forget₂ (ModuleCat R) Ab` via `isLimitOfReflects`. The `⊥`/sup open identities are exactly
8066	  %      the `h₂`/`h₁` fields of `Scheme.coprodPresheafObjIso`; the cone legs
8067	  %      `M.presheaf.map (homOfLE _).op` match the adjunction-unit components via
8068	  %      `restrictAdjunction_unit_app_app` (rfl). ~60–100 LOC of cone bookkeeping.
8069	  % The two private helpers `isIso_prodLift_of_isLimit` (prod.lift of a limit BinaryFan is iso, via
8070	  % `prodIsProd` + `IsLimit.conePointUniqueUpToIso_hom_comp`) and `coprodDecompMap` (the comparison
8071	  % map `M ⟶ inl_*(M|inl) ⨯ inr_*(M|inr)`, `prod.lift` of two `restrictAdjunction` units) are bundled
8072	  % into this lemma's `\lean{...}` list above so their dependency edges are carried.
8073	  Write \(q = \operatorname{coprod.desc}\,Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\,Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\). Build the module-linear
8074	  comparison map from \(\operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\operatorname{Over.mk} q)\) into the
8075	  binary product whose two components are the push--pull maps induced by the coproduct inclusions
8076	  \(\operatorname{inl} : Y_0.\mathrm{left} \to Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\) and
8077	  \(\operatorname{inr} : Y_1.\mathrm{left} \to Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\) (each an
8078	  arrow over \(X\), since \(q \circ \operatorname{inl} = Y_0.\mathrm{hom}\) and \(q \circ
8079	  \operatorname{inr} = Y_1.\mathrm{hom}\)). By Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf} it suffices
8080	  to check this comparison is an isomorphism on the underlying presheaf of abelian groups, i.e. on the
8081	  sections over each open \(V \subseteq X\).
8082	
8083	  Over a fixed \(V\), the sections of the left-hand side are the sections of \(q^*\mathcal{F}\) over the
8084	  preimage \(q^{-1}(V) \subseteq Y_0.\mathrm{left} \amalg Y_1.\mathrm{left}\). Inside the coproduct
8085	  space this preimage is the disjoint union of the two traces
8086	  \(\operatorname{inl}^{-1}(q^{-1}V)\) and \(\operatorname{inr}^{-1}(q^{-1}V)\) (disjoint because the two
8087	  coproduct summands are), so the binary disjoint-union sheaf decomposition --- sections over a binary
8088	  coproduct scheme are the product of the sections over the two preimages,
8089	  Lemma~\ref{lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib}, equivalently \(F(U \sqcup V') \cong F(U) \times F(V')\)
8090	  for disjoint opens, Lemma~\ref{lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib} --- identifies these sections with the
8091	  product of the sections of \(q^*\mathcal{F}\) over the two traces. By the over-triangle equalities the
8092	  trace sections are exactly \(\Gamma(V, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0)\) and
8093	  \(\Gamma(V, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_1)\). On the right-hand side, evaluation at \(V\)
8094	  preserves the binary product (Lemma~\ref{lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}), so its sections
8095	  are the same product \(\Gamma(V, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_0) \times \Gamma(V,
8096	  \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_1)\). The comparison realises precisely this identification on
8097	  every \(V\), hence is an isomorphism of presheaves of abelian groups, and the claim follows.
8098	\end{proof}
8099	
8100	\begin{lemma}
8101	[Push--pull on a finite coproduct of legs is the product of the leg push--pulls]
8102	  \label{lem:pushPull_coprod_prod}
8103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_coprod_prod}
8104	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}
8105	  Let \((\mathrm{legs} : \iota \to \operatorname{Over} X)\) be a finite family of objects of
8106	  the slice category over a scheme \(X\) (\(\iota\) finite), and form the coproduct
8107	  \(\coprod_{i \in \iota} \mathrm{legs}\,i\) with structure map
8108	  \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom}) : \coprod_i
8109	  \mathrm{legs}\,i \to X\). Then the push--pull object on this coproduct is the product of
8110	  the per-leg push--pull objects:
8111	  \[
8112	    \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,\Bigl(\operatorname{Over.mk}\bigl(
8113	      \operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\Bigr)
8114	      \;\cong\;
8115	    \prod_{i \in \iota} \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i)
8116	    \qquad \text{in } X.\mathrm{Modules}.
8117	  \]
8118	  This is the general indexed-coproduct\(\to\)product disjoint-union decomposition of a
8119	  module sheaf: the legs may have overlapping images in \(X\), but they are disjoint inside
8120	  the coproduct space \(\coprod_i \mathrm{legs}\,i\), so push--pull splits componentwise.
8121	\end{lemma}
8122	\begin{proof}
8123	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf, lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod}
8124	  Argue by induction on the finite index type \(\iota\), iterating the binary decomposition.
8125	
8126	  \emph{Empty base.} If \(\iota\) is empty, \(\coprod_i \mathrm{legs}\,i\) is the initial scheme;
8127	  pulling \(\mathcal{F}\) back and pushing forward along the (unique) initial structure map yields the
8128	  terminal object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), which is the empty product \(\prod_{i \in \emptyset}
8129	  \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i)\). The comparison map is an isomorphism on
8130	  the underlying presheaf of abelian groups (both sides have zero sections over every open), so by
8131	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf} it is an isomorphism in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
8132	
8133	  \emph{Inductive step.} Split off one index, writing \(\iota \simeq \{*\} \sqcup \iota'\) with
8134	  \(\iota'\) of strictly smaller cardinality. The coproduct \(\coprod_{i \in \iota} \mathrm{legs}\,i\)
8135	  reassociates as the binary coproduct of \(\mathrm{legs}\,*\) and \(\coprod_{i \in \iota'}
8136	  \mathrm{legs}\,i\), and the structure map \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \mapsto
8137	  (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom})\) becomes the \(\operatorname{coprod.desc}\) of \((\mathrm{legs}
8138	  \,*).\mathrm{hom}\) and the tail structure map \(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \in \iota' \mapsto
8139	  (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom})\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_binary_coprod_prod} the push--pull
8140	  object on this binary coproduct is the binary product of \(\operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,
8141	  (\mathrm{legs}\,*)\) and the push--pull object on the tail coproduct
8142	  \(\operatorname{Over.mk}(\operatorname{Sigma.desc}(i \in \iota' \mapsto (\mathrm{legs}\,i).\mathrm{hom}))\);
8143	  the induction hypothesis identifies the latter with \(\prod_{i \in \iota'}
8144	  \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,(\mathrm{legs}\,i)\). Composing the binary product with the
8145	  indexed product over \(\iota'\) gives the indexed product over \(\iota\), as claimed. (All
8146	  isomorphisms produced live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); where the reassociation of the coproduct is
8147	  needed it is verified through the underlying presheaf by
8148	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_modules_of_toPresheaf}.)
8149	\end{proof}

\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso}
  Both sides corepresent the functor \(\operatorname{sectionsFunctor}(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V)
  \,\circ\, \mathrm{forget}\): the object \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V)\) via the
  adjunction underlying the equivalence \(\Phi\), and the object
  \(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}\) via the defining corepresentability of
  \(\Gamma(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V,{-})\) by \(j_!\mathcal{O}\)
  (Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}). Both corepresent the same functor, so uniqueness of
  corepresenting objects identifies them.

  In detail, the argument is a corepresentability transport; \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\) is never unfolded.
  Write \(A = j_!\mathcal{O}_V\). We exhibit two corepresentations of one and the same
  \(\mathrm{Type}\)-valued functor and invoke the uniqueness-up-to-isomorphism of a corepresenting
  object to obtain the stated isomorphism, with no adjunction-mate transposition or naturality square
  computed by hand. The common functor is built by chaining three identifications.
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item The adjunction underlying the equivalence \(\Phi\) presents the functor corepresented by
      \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(A)\) as the composite of \(\Phi.\mathrm{inverse}\) with the functor
      corepresented by \(A\).
    \item The corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso}, whiskered by the
      forgetful functor to types, identifies the functor corepresented by \(A\) with the sections
      functor: it is \(\operatorname{sectionsFunctor} V\) as a \(\mathrm{Type}\)-valued functor. This
      rests on the corepresentability of \(\Gamma(V,-)\) by \(j_!\mathcal{O}_V\) of
      Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}.
    \item Sections of a pushforward are, by definition, sections over the preimage open:
      \(\Phi.\mathrm{inverse} \circ \operatorname{sectionsFunctor} V =
      \operatorname{sectionsFunctor}(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V)\), an equality of functors. Reading
      Lemma~\ref{lem:sectionsFunctorCorepIso} at the open \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V\) then exhibits
      this same functor as the one corepresented by \(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}\).
  \end{enumerate}
  Thus \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(A)\) and \(j_!\mathcal{O}_{\varphi.\mathrm{inv}^{-1}V}\) corepresent the
  same functor; the uniqueness of corepresenting objects up to isomorphism reflects this to the stated
  isomorphism of objects. The only geometric inputs are the two definitional equalities
  ``corepresented functor \(=\) sections functor'' and ``sections of a pushforward \(=\) sections over
  the preimage''.
\end{proof}

    lem:injective_cech_acyclic, lem:affine_cech_vanishing_general_of_tildeVanishing,
    lem:affine_cech_vanishing_general_seed, def:absolute_cohomology}
  Let \(R\) be a ring, \(V\) \emph{any} affine open of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), and
  \(\mathcal{H}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module. Then for every
  \(q \geq 1\) the absolute cohomology vanishes:
  \[
    H^q(V, \mathcal{H}) \;=\; \operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_V, \mathcal{H}) \;=\; 0.
  \]
  This is the general-affine-open companion of the distinguished-open Serre vanishing
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): the basis \(\mathcal{B}\) of the affine cover system is
  enlarged from the distinguished opens \(D(f)\) to all affine opens, while the admissible coverings
  \(\mathrm{Cov}\) remain the standard covers \(i \mapsto D(g_i)\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis, lem:affine_surj_of_vanishing_affine,
    lem:standard_cover_cofinal_affine, def:affine_cover_system_general, lem:affine_faces_mem,
    lem:injective_cech_acyclic, lem:affine_cech_vanishing_general_of_tildeVanishing,
    lem:affine_cech_vanishing_general_seed, def:absolute_cohomology}
    \leanok
  Instantiate the basis-comparison criterion (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) at the
  enlarged affine cover system (Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system_general}), whose basis
  \(\mathcal{B}\) is \emph{all} affine opens of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) and whose admissible coverings
  \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are the standard covers of an affine open \(V = \bigcup_i D(g_i)\). Its three
  cover-system fields are:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\): the faces of a standard cover are the distinguished opens
      \(\bigcap_k D(g_{\sigma k}) = D\bigl(\prod_k g_{\sigma k}\bigr)\), still distinguished and a
      fortiori affine, so they lie in the enlarged \(\mathcal{B}\) (Lemmas~\ref{lem:affine_faces_mem},
      \ref{lem:isAffineOpen_specBasicOpen}).
    \item \(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\): the family-form injective {\v C}ech-acyclicity
      (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}) is cover-agnostic and applies to the standard covers of
      any affine open verbatim.
    \item \(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\): the general-affine-open section surjectivity
      (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_surj_of_vanishing_affine}), whose refinement step
      (Lemma~\ref{lem:standard_cover_cofinal_affine}) uses the quasi-compactness of the affine open
      \(V\) in place of that of a distinguished \(D(f)\).
  \end{itemize}
  With \(\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{H}\) quasi-coherent, condition (3) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} (standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing) is \emph{not} the
  distinguished-open seed of Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}: a standard cover here covers a
  general affine open \(V = \bigcup_i D(g_i)\), whose coordinate ring \(\Gamma(V)\) is not a single
  localisation \(R_f\), so the \(D(f)\) seed does not apply. Condition (3) is instead supplied by the
  enlarged-system seed \(\operatorname{HasVanishingHigherCech}(\operatorname{affineCoverSystemGeneral}
  R)\, \mathcal{H}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_general_of_tildeVanishing}, which transports
  along \(\mathcal{H} \cong \widetilde{M}\) to the change-of-ring tilde seed
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_general_seed}). Reading off the conclusion at the member
  \(V \in \mathcal{B}\) gives \(H^q(V, \mathcal{H}) = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\); the identification
  \(H^q(V, -) = \operatorname{Ext}^q(j_!\mathcal{O}_V, -)\) is the Ext realization of
  Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Ext transport along the whole-scheme spectrum equivalence (Need~\#1)]
  \label{lem:modules_isoSpec_ext_transport}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.modulesIsoSpecExtTransport,
    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pushforwardEquivOfIso,
    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardEquivOfIso_functor_additive,
    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pushforwardExtAddEquiv}
  \uses{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib, lem:ext_mapExactFunctor_mathlib,
    lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
  Let \(U\) be a scheme with \([\,\mathrm{IsAffine}\ U\,]\), let \(V \subseteq U\) be an affine open,
  and let \(\mathcal{H}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module. The whole-scheme isomorphism
  \(U \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib})
  induces, for every \(q\), an isomorphism
  \[
    \operatorname{Ext}^q_{U}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_V,\, \mathcal{H}\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
    \operatorname{Ext}^q_{\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'},\, \mathcal{H}'\bigr),
  \]
  where \(V'\) is the affine open image of \(V\) under the isomorphism and \(\mathcal{H}'\) is the
  transported (still quasi-coherent) coefficient. In particular the residual over \(U\) vanishes iff
  its transport over \(\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U\) does.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib, lem:ext_mapExactFunctor_mathlib,
    lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib, lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso,
    lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh, lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}
  Since \(U\) is affine, \(\operatorname{Scheme.isoSpec}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib})
  gives a genuine isomorphism of schemes \(\varphi : U \cong \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U)\).
  Assemble from the pushforward functors and their coherences
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_pushforward_mathlib}) the equivalence of module categories
  \[
    \Phi = \operatorname{pushforwardEquivOfIso}\varphi :
      U.\mathrm{Modules} \;\simeq\; (\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U).\mathrm{Modules},
  \]
  with \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}\) the pushforward along \(\varphi.\mathrm{hom}\) and its quasi-inverse the
  pushforward along \(\varphi.\mathrm{inv}\); the unit and counit are supplied by the composition and
  identity coherences. An equivalence is exact and additive, so
  \(\operatorname{Ext.mapExactFunctor}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_mapExactFunctor_mathlib}) applies and
  yields a map on \(\operatorname{Ext}\); since \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}\), being one half of an
  equivalence, preserves injective objects, its induced \(\operatorname{Ext}\)-map is bijective
  (using enough injectives on \(U.\mathrm{Modules}\)), giving an additive isomorphism. This gives
  the displayed isomorphism once the two arguments are identified. For the first
  argument, a scheme isomorphism carries the corepresenting object along
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_transport_along_iso}): \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(j_!\mathcal{O}_V) \cong
  j_!\mathcal{O}_{V'}\), where \(V' = \varphi.\mathrm{hom}\,{''}\,V\) is the image, an affine open of
  \(\operatorname{Spec}\Gamma U\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:isAffineOpen_image_of_iso_mathlib}. For the second,
  \(\Phi.\mathrm{functor}(\mathcal{H}) = \mathcal{H}'\) is again quasi-coherent because pushforward along
  an isomorphism preserves quasi-coherence (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_iso_preserves_qcoh}).
\end{proof}
